description:
Authors: Zhou, K. and Zhu, Y. and Chen, Z. and Chen, W. and Zhao, W. X. and Chen, X. and Lin, Y. and Wen, J.-R. and Han, J.
Title: Don't make your LLM an evaluation benchmark cheater
Year: 2023
URL: URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.01964
reason: Paper confirmed via arXiv identifier arXiv:2311.01964 and multiple web sources with matching title, authors, and year. Despite no Semantic Scholar result, the arXiv record and consistent metadata confirm authenticity.
prompt_variant: You are a critical academic research verifier. Your task: determine if a bibliog...
_search_results:
This paper **exists** and is a published arXiv preprint.[1][5]

**Exact Title:** "Don't Make Your LLM an Evaluation Benchmark Cheater"[5]

**Authors:** Kun Zhou, Yutao Zhu, Zhipeng Chen, Wentong Chen, Wayne Xin Zhao, Xu Chen, Yankai Lin, Ji-Rong Wen, Jiawei Han[5]

**Venue:** arXiv (Computer Science > Computation and Language)[5]

**Year:** 2023[1]

**DOI/Identifier:** arXiv:2311.01964[5]

**Submission Date:** November 3, 2023[5]

The paper addresses **benchmark leakage** in large language model evaluation—the problem of evaluation datasets inadvertently overlapping with training data, which artificially inflates performance metrics and compromises fair model comparison.[5] The authors conduct experiments demonstrating that data contamination can dramatically boost evaluation results while harming generalization, and provide guidelines for both LLM developers and benchmark maintainers.[2]
Semantic Scholar: no matching paper found.

**Yes, the paper exists.**

- **Exact title**: Don't Make Your LLM an Evaluation Benchmark Cheater[1][2][3][5]
- **Authors**: Kun Zhou, Yutao Zhu, Zhipeng Chen, Wentong Chen, Wayne Xin Zhao, Xu Chen, Yankai Lin, Ji-Rong Wen, Jiawei Han[1][3][5]
- **Venue**: arXiv preprint arXiv:2311.01964 (cs.CL)[3][4]
- **Year**: 2023 (submitted November 3, 2023)[1][3][4]
- **DOI/URL**: arXiv:2311.01964 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.01964)[3]; PDF at https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.01964[4]; Semantic Scholar CorpusID: 265019021[5]

The paper examines **benchmark leakage** in LLM evaluation, where training data overlaps with test sets, leading to inflated scores and unreliable assessments (e.g., smaller models outperforming larger ones on leaked tasks).[2][3][4] It includes experiments on leakage effects and provides guidelines for LLM developers and benchmark maintainers to avoid this issue.[2][3]
